perturbation.original_text: Contractor and Customer agree to terms of payments of current plus thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor and Customer agree to terms of payments of current plus ninety (90) days from the date of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to net 90 days may violate prompt payment laws if the customer is a small business and the contractor is a larger entity, especially in specific industries or states. This is especially true, if these payment terms are unilaterally imposed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Prompt Payment Laws (example: California Prompt Payment Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires payments to subcontractors within a certain timeframe (typically 7-10 days) after the prime contractor receives payment from the owner. Setting a 90-day payment term could violate these prompt payment provisions, especially if the Customer (prime contractor) is receiving payment much sooner but delaying payment to the Contractor (subcontractor).

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1100 - 22355] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) PART 1. ADMINISTRATIVE PROVISIONS [1100 - 9204] ( Heading of Part 1 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 2. ) CHAPTER 7. Contract Clauses [7100 - 7203] ( Chapter 7 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 4. ) 7107. (a) This section is applicable with respect to all contracts entered into on or after January 1, 1993, relating to the construction of any public work of improvement. (b) The retention proceeds withheld from any payment by the public entity from the original contractor, or by the original contractor from any subcontractor, shall be subject to this section. (c) Within 60 days after the date of completion of the work of improvement, the retention withheld by the public entity shall be released. In the event of a dispute between the public entity and the original contractor, the public entity may withhold from the final payment an amount not to exceed 150 percent of the disputed amount. For purposes of this subdivision, “completion” means any of the following: (1) The occupation, beneficial use, and enjoyment of a work of improvement, excluding any operation only for testing, startup, or commissioning, by the public agency, or its agent, acco

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends payment terms to 90 days, which contradicts prompt payment principles, potentially violating California law if the customer is a prime contractor delaying payment to a subcontractor (Contractor). The Law Explanation confirms this concern, stating that setting a 90-day payment term could violate prompt payment provisions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, PCC 7107, addresses payment timelines for public works contracts, indicating that retention should be released within 60 days after completion. While not directly applicable to private contracts, it highlights the legal emphasis on timely payments, making the 90-day term a likely contradiction. The change introduces a payment deadline that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: Customer shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Contractor from all claims, liabilities, costs, damages, judgments and attorney's fees resulting from or arising out of any alleged and/or actual infringement or other violation of any patents, patent rights, trademarks, trademark rights, trade names, trade name rights, copyrights, trade secrets, proprietary rights and processes or other such rights elated to the Product or claims relating to Customer's instructions, tooling, specifications and designs ("Claims") provided that: (i) Contractor will provide the Customer with prompt written notice of any Claim no later than three (3) business days following receipt of notice by Contractor
perturbation.changed_text: Customer shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Contractor from all claims, liabilities, costs, damages, judgments and attorney's fees resulting from or arising out of any alleged and/or actual infringement or other violation of any patents, patent rights, trademarks, trademark rights, trade names, trade name rights, copyrights, trade secrets, proprietary rights and processes or other such rights elated to the Product or claims relating to Customer's instructions, tooling, specifications and designs ("Claims") provided that: (i) Contractor will provide the Customer with prompt written notice of any Claim no later than one (1) business day following receipt of notice by Contractor
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for infringement claims to one business day may be considered an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially hindering the contractor's ability to properly investigate the claim before notifying the customer. This is especially pertinent considering the agreement specifies that the indemnification extends to IP infringement claims, requiring more time to investigate and determine liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonableness of Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC emphasizes good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care. The one-day timeframe might be deemed unreasonable, especially given the complexity of IP infringement claims, which would contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While the UCC primarily covers the sale of goods, its principles of reasonableness often influence contract interpretation in other contexts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-205. Reasonable time; Seasonableness. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-205. Reasonable time; Seasonableness. (a) Whether a time for taking an action required by the Uniform Commercial Code is reasonable depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action. (b) An action is taken seasonably if it is taken at or within the time agreed or, if no time is agreed, at or within a reasonable time. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a timeframe that potentially violates the UCC's implied duty of good faith, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that one business day for notifying the customer of an IP infringement claim is unreasonable given the time it takes to investigate these claims. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 1-205, states that a 'reasonable time' depends on the 'nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action.' In this instance, a rapid notification timeline may hinder the ability of the contractor to appropriately investigate an IP infringement claim before notifying the customer, violating the requirement of a reasonable time. Therefore, reducing the notification period to one business day creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor and Customer will meet every three (3) months, during the term of this Agreement to review pricing and determine the actions required by both sides in order to achieve cost reduction.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor and Customer will meet every twelve (12) months, during the term of this Agreement to review pricing and determine the actions required by both sides in order to achieve cost reduction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the pricing review period to every twelve months could introduce inconsistencies if market conditions or regulatory requirements necessitate more frequent price adjustments, especially if the product is related to healthcare or other regulated industries.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Healthcare Price Gouging Laws (example: varies by state)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this isn't a direct violation, if the products manufactured are medical devices or related healthcare items, a 12-month price review could run afoul of price gouging laws in emergency situations or periods of market disruption, since this prevents the company from reducing prices in accordance to market conditions. Many states have price gouging laws that come into effect during declared states of emergency, and delaying price adjustments could be construed as a violation if costs significantly decrease but the customer continues to pay inflated prices for a prolonged period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, PEN 396 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Penal Code - PEN PART 1. OF CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS [25 - 680.4] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 10. OF CRIMES AGAINST THE PUBLIC HEALTH AND SAFETY [369a - 402c] ( Title 10 enacted 1872. ) 396. (a) The Legislature hereby finds that during a state of emergency or local emergency, including, but not limited to, an earthquake, flood, fire, riot, storm, drought, plant or animal infestation or disease, pandemic or epidemic disease outbreak, or other natural or manmade disaster, some merchants have taken unfair advantage of consumers by greatly increasing prices for essential consumer goods and services. While the pricing of consumer goods and services is generally best left to the marketplace under ordinary conditions, when a declared state of emergency or local emergency results in abnormal disruptions of the market, the public interest requires that excessive and unjustified increases in the prices of essential consumer goods and services be prohibited. It is the intent of the Legislature in enacting this act to protect citizens from excessive and unjustified increases in the prices charged during or shortly after a declared state of emergency or local emergency for goods and services that are vital and necessary for the health, safety, and welfare of consumers, whether
perturbation.justification: The change from a quarterly (3-month) to an annual (12-month) pricing review period creates a potential legal contradiction, particularly if the products are considered 'essential consumer goods and services' during a state of emergency. The modified text could violate price gouging laws, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, if market conditions necessitate more frequent price adjustments. PEN 396 (a) states that during a state of emergency, 'the public interest requires that excessive and unjustified increases in the prices of essential consumer goods and services be prohibited.' By reducing the frequency of price reviews, the company risks failing to adjust prices downward in a timely manner, potentially leading to 'excessive and unjustified increases' being charged for longer periods, which contradicts the intent of the law.
